steps.title: A fraction equals zero when its numerator is zero
steps.content:
A rational expression equals zero only when its numerator is zero (and the denominator is non-zero). Therefore:
$$\frac{(x-7)(x-9)}{x-4}=0 \implies (x-7)(x-9) = 0$$
with the restriction $x \neq 4$.

steps.title: Extract solutions from the factorization and compute their sum
steps.content: From $(x-7)(x-9) = 0$, the solutions are $x = 7$ and $x = 9$. Both satisfy $x \neq 4$ (denominators are $3$ and $5$, respectively). The sum of the solutions is $7 + 9 = 16$. That matches choice C — C is correct.
